title: Replacing a basin tap
subtitle: Isolate the supply, disconnect the old tap, fit the new one
excerpt: How to replace a dripping or seized basin tap: isolating the water supply, disconnecting the flexible tail from below the basin, removing the old tap body, fitting the new tap, reconnecting the supply, and testing.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Plumbase and City Plumbing technical guides (public-facing). BS 6700 specification for water supply systems.

content.text: Replacing a basin tap is among the most common plumbing jobs in a domestic bathroom. The awkward part is not the plumbing connection but the physical access: the
back-nut

is underneath the basin in a confined space. A basin wrench, designed to reach into the tight area between the basin underside and the floor, makes the job possible; without one, it can take several hours of awkward work.
Step 1, isolate the supply, drain the section, catch the residual

Close the service valve on the supply pipe below the basin; most modern basins have a 1/4-turn isolating valve on both hot and cold tails. If there is no local valve, close the stopcock under the kitchen sink (cold) or the gate valve on the hot-water draw-off (hot). Open the tap you are replacing and run it until the flow stops. Have a bowl and cloth below the basin to catch the residual water in the flex connector and the tap body.
Disconnecting the old tap
Step 2, disconnect the flex connector.
The
flexible tap connector
runs from the tap tail downward to the supply pipe. Unscrew the upper nut from the tap tail first, then the lower nut from the service valve or pipe fitting. Have the bowl ready for residual water.
Step 3, remove the back-nut.

Lie on your back under the basin (or kneel on the floor below the pedestal). Insert the basin wrench onto the back-nut and turn anticlockwise to loosen. If the nut is plastic (common on older taps), use the basin wrench only; do not use a pipe wrench on a plastic nut as it will crack. Once loose, spin the nut off by hand.
Step 4, lift out the old tap.
Lift the tap body up and out from the top of the basin. Old sealing washers may stick to the basin face; clean away any old rubber or silicone sealant.
Fitting the new tap
Step 5, prepare the new tap.

Fit the tap through the basin hole from above. Fit the plastic washer and then the back-nut onto the tap tail from below. Hand-tighten the back-nut, then give it a further quarter-turn with the basin wrench. Do not overtighten; ceramic basins can crack.
Step 6, connect the flex connector.

Fit a new flexible connector (replace the existing one; they can fail after disturbance). Tighten the upper nut onto the tap tail and the lower nut onto the service valve. Most flexible connectors have a rubber seated washer that seals without thread sealant; if there is no washer, wrap the male threads with 5 turns of PTFE tape clockwise.
Step 7, restore and test.
Open the service valve slowly. Check all connections for drips with the tap closed, then open the tap. Check again. Allow to run for 30 seconds and re-check.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Close the isolation valve and tighten the nut by a quarter-turn. If the drip continues, isolate, disconnect, replace the washer, and reconnect.
attrs.cause: Nut not fully tightened, or washer missing or damaged.
attrs.symptom: Drip from the flex connector joint after refilling

attrs.fix: Apply penetrating oil (WD-40 or similar) to the thread, allow to soak for 30 minutes, then try again. If still stuck, a hacksaw blade can cut through a plastic back-nut from below.
attrs.cause: Corrosion between the nut and the tap tail on an old installation.
